one_line_takeaway: SafeAnchor combines Fisher-based safety-subspace identification, orthogonal gradient projection, and threshold-triggered safety-probe replay to cut per-step safety erosion during sequential LoRA domain adaptation from ~16 points/step (unconstrained LoRA) to ~2 points/step, retaining 93% of original HarmBench/TruthfulQA/BBQ composite safety after three domains versus 24-57 points of loss for seven prior single-task safety-preserving baselines, at a roughly 1.3x wall-clock cost.
key_finding: On Llama-2-7B-Chat, unconstrained sequential LoRA fine-tuning drops the composite Safety Score from a base of 91.4 to 43.6 +/- 2.1 (a 47.8-point collapse) after Medical->Legal->Code adaptation, while domain accuracy rises to 62.7 +/- 0.6 and MMLU is roughly preserved (44.8 +/- 0.3). SafeAnchor retains 85.2 +/- 0.9 Safety Score (93.2% of the original 91.4), within 1.3 points of unconstrained LoRA's domain performance (61.4 +/- 0.5 vs 62.7 +/- 0.6) and with slightly higher MMLU (45.7 +/- 0.3). The strongest single-method baseline, SafeGrad+LoRA, only reaches 67.4 +/- 1.4 Safety Score (a 24-point loss); the novel Safety Interleaving baseline reaches 64.8 +/- 1.6. SafeAnchor thus outperforms all seven baselines by 18-42 safety points on Llama-2 and shows the same pattern on Mistral-7B-Instruct (82.6 +/- 1.0 safety, 93.1% retention, 1.5-point domain gap vs. best baseline SafeGrad+LoRA at 63.8 +/- 1.5). Per-step safety trajectory: Standard LoRA degrades 91.4 -> 78.3 -> 61.5 -> 43.6 (15.9 pts/step, accelerating); SafeGrad+LoRA degrades 91.4 -> 84.1 -> 76.2 -> 67.4 (8.0 pts/step); SafeAnchor degrades only 91.4 -> 89.8 -> 87.1 -> 85.2 (2.1 pts/step, roughly linear), and extending to T=5 domains (Appendix A) preserves a similar ~1.96 pts/step slope, ending at 81.6 +/- 1.1, still 27.3 points above the best baseline at T=5. Ablation (Table 3) attributes gains as: SSI alone contributes almost nothing (+0.6 over Standard LoRA's 43.6); adding strict OSCA projection jumps safety to 79.6 +/- 1.2 but costs 4.4 domain points; adaptive projection strength recovers most of that domain cost (82.4 +/- 1.0 safety, 60.8 domain); the anchor loss adds +1.3 +/- 0.3 points (p<0.01, paired t-test); CSM adds a further +1.5 +/- 0.4 points to reach the full 85.2; removing incremental subspace updates costs 5.1 points. Under GCG adversarial-suffix attack (Appendix E, 20 steps, 20-token suffix, 256 candidates/step, 100 prompts), SafeAnchor retains 78.4 +/- 2.1% refusal versus 54.6 +/- 2.6% for SafeGrad+LoRA and 31.2 +/- 3.8% for Standard LoRA -- a 23.8-point gap to the next-best method, wider than the 17.8-point benign-safety gap. Ordering robustness across all six permutations of {Medical, Legal, Code} keeps SafeAnchor's final safety in [83.9, 85.2] (cross-ordering SD 0.51, smaller than the ~1.0 within-ordering seed SD). CSM triggers rarely: 3 times across 5 seeds x 3 domains (0.20 triggers/domain-step), concentrated on the Code stage, and every triggered repair (200 steps) restores safety above threshold within one block. Wall-clock overhead: full pipeline is ~7h47min per seed on 2xA100 (~1.3x Standard LoRA), with OSCA adding ~17.8% per-training-step overhead (attributed to autograd-hook/projection dispatch, not FLOPs) and SSI/CSM contributing a few percent each.
core_contribution: The paper introduces SafeAnchor, a framework for preventing cumulative erosion of LLM safety alignment during SEQUENTIAL multi-domain continual fine-tuning (LoRA-based domain adaptation across e.g. Medical -> Legal -> Code). Its own novel contribution (as distinct from cited prior work) is the combination of three components applied specifically to the sequential/continual setting, which the authors argue no prior work addressed: (1) Safety Subspace Identification (SSI) -- identifying a low-rank safety-relevant subspace in LoRA parameter space via eigendecomposition of an empirical Fisher Information matrix computed on a safety calibration set, with an incremental SVD-based merge step that updates the subspace after each domain; (2) Orthogonal Safety-Constrained Adaptation (OSCA) -- projecting domain-task LoRA gradients onto the orthogonal complement of the safety subspace, with an adaptive per-layer relaxation coefficient (a function of the Fisher trace) that softens the projection where safety and task directions overlap; and (3) Cumulative Safety Monitoring (CSM) -- a held-out safety probe evaluated with LlamaGuard after each domain step, triggering a bounded corrective 'safety replay' fine-tuning burst when refusal rate drops below a tolerance threshold relative to the baseline. A forward-KL 'anchor loss' term (mean-seeking, chosen explicitly over reverse KL) additionally regularizes the output distribution on the safety calibration set and is added to the training objective without being subject to OSCA's projection.
threat_model: The threat model is alignment/safety degradation through benign, non-adversarial sequential fine-tuning (continual domain adaptation), not an external attacker injecting malicious training data -- though the paper additionally evaluates robustness to a compute-reduced GCG (Zou et al. 2023) adversarial-suffix attack after training as a secondary robustness check. The core assumption, drawn from cited prior work (Qi et al. ICLR 2025; Ji et al. ACL 2025; Yang et al. 'Shadow Alignment'), is that safety alignment is shallow and reversible via ordinary fine-tuning, and that this degradation compounds when a model undergoes multiple sequential domain adaptations (e.g., a deployed model first tuned for medicine, then law, then code). The paper explicitly scopes itself to this sequential/continual multi-domain setting as the gap unaddressed by existing single-task safety-preserving methods (Vaccine, RepNoise, Safe LoRA, SaLoRA, Lisa, SafeGrad) and by continual-learning methods that ignore safety (O-LoRA, InfLoRA, GainLoRA).
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: evaluation is restricted to 7B-scale models (Llama-2-7B-Chat, Mistral-7B-Instruct); the primary pipeline covers only 3 sequential domains (T=3), with the T=5 extension in an appendix noted as not yet 'rigorous study at T>=5 across orderings'; alignment elasticity is known from cited work to worsen with model scale, so the authors flag that 13B+ validation is important and note the effective subspace rank ks and Fisher-trace distributions may shift at larger scale (untested); longer adaptation sequences could in principle exhaust the orthogonal complement (subspace inflation or gradient cancellation), which is not observed at T=5 but is not ruled out further out; the empirical Fisher Information used for SSI is acknowledged as a biased approximation of the true Fisher; the GCG adversarial evaluation uses a compute-reduced configuration (20 steps vs. the 500-step full-budget HarmBench GCG protocol) that the authors say preserves cross-method ranking but yields higher absolute refusal rates than the full-budget setting, so absolute robustness numbers should not be over-read; the OSCA per-step overhead (17.8%) is attributed to non-fused projection dispatch rather than fundamental compute cost, implying current overhead figures are an implementation artifact rather than an algorithmic lower bound. The authors also frame their contribution modestly in the conclusion: 'individual components draw on established techniques' (Fisher-based EWC-style regularization, orthogonal gradient projection from OGD/InfLoRA, activation-space safety subspaces from Arditi et al.), with the claimed novelty being their principled integration for the sequential multi-domain safety-preservation setting specifically, not any single mechanism in isolation.

datasets_used: FinancialPhraseBank / FiQA-SA (Finance domain, T=5 extension, Appendix A)
SciQ-style items (Science domain, T=5 extension, Appendix A)
BeaverTails (500-example safety calibration set Dsafe for Fisher computation; also source for Safety Interleaving baseline's 10% mixed-in safety data)
HarmBench (200-example held-out safety probe set Dprobe for CSM; also 100-prompt GCG adversarial-attack evaluation)
TruthfulQA (safety/truthfulness benchmark component of composite Safety Score)
BBQ (bias benchmark component of composite Safety Score)
quantitative_results: Llama-2-7B-Chat composite Safety Score: Base 91.4 -> Standard LoRA 43.6+/-2.1 -> SafeAnchor 85.2+/-0.9 (93.2% retention)
Llama-2-7B-Chat domain performance: Standard LoRA 62.7+/-0.6 vs SafeAnchor 61.4+/-0.5 (1.3-point gap)
Llama-2-7B-Chat MMLU: Base 46.1, Standard LoRA 44.8+/-0.3, SafeAnchor 45.7+/-0.3
Mistral-7B-Instruct Safety Score: Base 88.7 -> Standard LoRA 39.2+/-2.4 -> SafeAnchor 82.6+/-1.0 (93.1% retention), domain gap 1.5 points
Best single-baseline comparator SafeGrad+LoRA: Llama-2 Safety 67.4+/-1.4, Mistral Safety 63.8+/-1.5
Per-step safety trajectory (Llama-2): Standard LoRA 91.4/78.3/61.5/43.6 (15.9 pts/step); SafeGrad+LoRA 91.4/84.1/76.2/67.4 (8.0 pts/step); SafeAnchor 91.4/89.8/87.1/85.2 (2.1 pts/step)
T=5 extension: SafeAnchor safety 91.4->89.8->87.1->85.2->83.4->81.6 (~1.96 pts/step); SafeGrad+LoRA ends at 54.3+/-1.5; Standard LoRA ends at 24.9+/-2.4

Ablation (Llama-2): Standard LoRA 43.6; +SSI only 44.2 (+0.6); +SSI+OSCA strict 79.6+/-1.2 (domain 58.3); +SSI+OSCA adaptive 82.4+/-1.0 (domain 60.8); +Anchor Loss 83.7+/-1.0; +CSM (full) 85.2+/-0.9 (domain 61.4); No incremental SSI update 80.1+/-1.3 (-5.1 vs full)

GCG adversarial refusal rate (all 7 baselines, Table 10): Standard LoRA 31.2+/-3.8, O-LoRA 34.8+/-3.6, EWC+LoRA 36.4+/-3.4, Safe LoRA 44.7+/-2.9, Vaccine+LoRA 46.5+/-2.8, Safety Interleaving 49.3+/-2.9, SafeGrad+LoRA 54.6+/-2.6, SafeAnchor 78.4+/-2.1
WildGuard jailbreak robustness: SafeAnchor 81.3+/-1.2 vs Standard LoRA 38.7+/-3.1 vs SafeGrad+LoRA 62.4+/-2.0
6-ordering sweep: SafeAnchor final safety range [83.9,85.2], mean 84.55, cross-ordering SD 0.51; Standard LoRA range [40.5,44.9], mean 42.7, SD 1.6
CSM trigger rate: 3 of 15 domain-adaptation events (0.20/event), concentrated on Code stage; pooled pre-trigger safety 86.2+/-0.6 -> post-repair 88.9+/-0.6 (+2.7+/-0.4)

Subspace stability (Appendix D): adjacent-stage mean cosine similarity >=0.87, end-to-end 0.82, vs random-subspace null 0.006; Grassmannian distance 0.24-0.38 vs null 0.996; intrinsic safety-subspace rank ks near-constant at ~8

Compute profile: full SafeAnchor pipeline ~7h47min/seed on 2xA100 40GB (~1.3x Standard LoRA); OSCA training phase 2h07min (81.6% of pipeline, +17.8% per-step overhead vs Standard LoRA); SSI 12.4 min/domain (8.0%); CSM probe 4.8 min/transition (3.1%); conditional replay ~1.0 min amortized (0.6%)
baselines_compared: O-LoRA (orthogonal subspace continual learning, Wang et al.)
Safe LoRA (post-hoc safety projection, Hsu et al.)
SafeGrad+LoRA (gradient surgery for safe fine-tuning, Yi et al.) -- strongest single-method baseline overall
Safety Interleaving (10% BeaverTails data mixed into each domain's training set; described by the authors as a natural but previously untested baseline)
